sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports a history of bilateral knee pain, with the right knee being more prominent, stemming from a skiing injury two weeks prior. He describes skiing downhill and colliding with a tree. He states his left knee doesn't hurt much, but the right knee is painful. He reports feeling unstable in his right knee. He rates his pain as a 7/10. He experiences pain when walking for longer periods, particularly during his dog walks, and it can reach an 11/10 when doing so. He has been wearing a brace and using THC cream for the right knee. He denies any current medications other than THC gummies from his grandmother.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: Physical exam reveals mild swelling and bruising on the right knee. Gait is slightly limp. Range of motion is intact, though with some stiffness in the morning. Dorsiflexion causes pain, while plantar flexion does not. The patient can bend both knees. There is no palpable tenderness to palpation on the left knee.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's symptoms are consistent with a posterior cruciate ligament strain and possible early osteoarthritis. The history of previous knee surgery and a prior injury contribute to the current presentation.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: I recommend continuing to brace the right knee for support. I am recommending physical therapy three times per week to improve strength and range of motion. I am prescribing meloxicam 15mg once daily for pain and inflammation, and ibuprofen 800mg twice daily as needed for the left knee. I advise against skiing or gymnastics for now. Schedule a follow-up in two weeks to assess progress.
